threats.title: SgxVerifier addressRegistered Not Updated During Instance Replacement Allows Duplicate Registration
threats.hypothesis_description: When SgxVerifier._replaceInstance is called during proof verification, the newInstance address is NOT added to the addressRegistered mapping, nor is the oldInstance address cleared. This allows the newInstance address to pass the addressRegistered check in _addInstances and obtain a second, independent instance ID via registerInstance(), bypassing the one-address-one-instance invariant.
threats.investigation_rationale: The addressRegistered mapping is specifically designed to prevent an address from being registered multiple times. The gap in _replaceInstance means an SGX enclave can maintain multiple valid instance IDs, potentially circumventing the 180-day expiry by rotating between them.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _replaceInstance sets instances[id] = Instance(newInstance, ...) but never sets addressRegistered[newInstance] = true

preconditions: SGX enclave has a registered instance that has been used for proof verification (triggering _replaceInstance)
Automata DCAP attestation service is available (for registerInstance path)
The newInstance address generated after replacement has not been previously registered via _addInstances

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: After replacement, addressRegistered[newInstance] is false, allowing registerInstance to create a duplicate entry
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No addressRegistered update in _replaceInstance; no addressRegistered[oldInstance] = false either

analysis_questions: Can the same SGX enclave address be registered under two different instance IDs after key rotation?
Can an attacker use duplicate instance IDs to bypass expiry checks?
Does the old instance's addressRegistered entry prevent legitimate re-attestation?
validation_requirements: Demonstrate that after verifyProof -> _replaceInstance, the newInstance can call registerInstance to get a second ID
Verify that two valid instance IDs for same address allow indefinite key reuse
Check if old instance address being permanently registered blocks re-attestation after deletion

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: 1) SGX enclave proves a block, _replaceInstance rotates oldAddr->newAddr. 2) addressRegistered[newAddr] remains false. 3) Enclave calls registerInstance with valid attestation for newAddr. 4) _addInstances passes check (addressRegistered[newAddr] == false), creating second instance ID. 5) Enclave now has two valid IDs, can bypass 180-day expiry by alternating.
prerequisites: Active SGX instance that has been used for proof verification

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: SGX key expiry bypass undermines side-channel protection model; a compromised SGX key could be used indefinitely
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All protocol users relying on SGX proof integrity
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Owner can delete instances via deleteInstances, but detection is difficult

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for duplicate registration after instance replacement

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Can be detected by monitoring InstanceAdded events for duplicate addresses

threats.title: GuardianProver Approval Hash Excludes proof.data Allowing Last Guardian to Control RETURN_LIVENESS_BOND
threats.hypothesis_description: In GuardianProver.approve(), the approval hash is computed as keccak256(abi.encode(_meta, _tran)) which does NOT include _proof. All guardians approve the same (meta, tran) pair, but the guardian whose approval triggers the threshold determines the actual _proof.data passed to TaikoL1.proveBlock(). A malicious guardian can set proof.data to the RETURN_LIVENESS_BOND magic value (keccak256('RETURN_LIVENESS_BOND')), causing the liveness bond to be returned to the assigned prover, which other guardians may not have intended.
threats.investigation_rationale: The proof.data parameter has security-critical side effects when it equals RETURN_LIVENESS_BOND. Since the approval hash doesn't bind all guardians to the same proof.data, the last guardian has unilateral control over this behavior.

proof_of_signal.pattern: hash = keccak256(abi.encode(_meta, _tran)) excludes _proof; last guardian's _proof.data forwarded to TaikoL1.proveBlock

preconditions: At least minGuardians guardians approve the same (meta, tran) pair
The last guardian to meet threshold is malicious or colluding with the assigned prover
The block has a non-zero livenessBond

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: _proof parameter not included in approval hash but controls RETURN_LIVENESS_BOND behavior
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Different guardians can submit different proof.data but all count toward the same approval threshold
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No mechanism to ensure all guardians agreed on the same proof.data

analysis_questions: Can the triggering guardian pass proof.data = RETURN_LIVENESS_BOND while other guardians intended standard proof data?
What is the economic impact of unauthorized RETURN_LIVENESS_BOND triggering?
Could this be used to systematically drain protocol funds by colluding with assigned provers?
validation_requirements: Confirm that proof.data is not part of the approval hash
Trace the RETURN_LIVENESS_BOND path through LibProving to verify it transfers livenessBond to assignedProver
Verify that after RETURN_LIVENESS_BOND, blk.livenessBond is zeroed preventing double recovery

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: 1) Honest guardians approve (meta, tran) with standard proof.data. 2) Malicious guardian approves last with proof.data = RETURN_LIVENESS_BOND (32 bytes). 3) Threshold met, malicious guardian's call triggers TaikoL1.proveBlock with RETURN_LIVENESS_BOND. 4) Liveness bond (250 TKO) transferred to assignedProver. 5) Malicious guardian colludes with assignedProver who should have lost the bond.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: 250 TKO per block improperly returned to assigned provers; systematic exploitation scales linearly
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Protocol treasury and honest provers who would otherwise receive the bond
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Difficult to detect on-chain; requires monitoring proof.data in guardian proof submissions

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for guardians submitting different proof.data for same approval hash

technical_investigation_focus.capital_requirements: None - guardian has no bond requirement
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable by comparing proof.data across guardian approval txns

threats.title: Proposer Can Steal ERC20 Tokens via Arbitrary Coinbase in AssignmentHook Fee Payment
threats.hypothesis_description: In AssignmentHook.onBlockProposed(), ERC20 prover fee payments use safeTransferFrom(_meta.coinbase, _blk.assignedProver, proverFee). The proposer controls params.coinbase which becomes meta.coinbase. The prover's assignment signature (hashAssignment) does NOT include the coinbase address. A malicious proposer can set coinbase to any address that has approved the AssignmentHook for the feeToken, causing the victim's tokens to be used for the prover fee payment.
threats.investigation_rationale: The coinbase parameter controls who pays ERC20 prover fees but is not part of the prover's signed assignment. Any proposer who has previously approved AssignmentHook for an ERC20 feeToken is vulnerable to having their tokens stolen by another proposer.

proof_of_signal.pattern: safeTransferFrom(_meta.coinbase, _blk.assignedProver, proverFee) where coinbase is proposer-controlled and not part of hashAssignment

preconditions: Victim address has approved AssignmentHook for the specific feeToken (ERC20)
Attacker has a valid prover assignment using that feeToken
feeToken is not address(0) (ETH payment path is different)
AssignmentHook is used as the hook in proposeBlock

initial_code_evidence.suspicious_location: AssignmentHook.sol lines 109-112 (ERC20 fee payment) and lines 130-145 (hashAssignment excludes coinbase)
initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: coinbase not included in signed assignment hash; proposer-controlled coinbase used as token source
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: _meta.coinbase is set by proposer in params.coinbase; any address with approval can be drained
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No validation that meta.coinbase == proposer or that coinbase is part of signed data

analysis_questions: Is coinbase intentionally excluded from the assignment hash or is this an oversight?
How common are ERC20 approvals to AssignmentHook in practice?
Can a proposer use unlimited approvals to drain all of a victim's feeToken balance?
validation_requirements: Confirm hashAssignment does not include coinbase
Demonstrate that proposer can set coinbase to arbitrary address in params
Verify safeTransferFrom succeeds when victim has approved AssignmentHook

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: 1) Victim (proposer A) approves AssignmentHook for USDC with large allowance. 2) Attacker (proposer B) obtains valid prover assignment with feeToken=USDC. 3) Attacker calls proposeBlock with params.coinbase = victim's address. 4) AssignmentHook.onBlockProposed executes safeTransferFrom(victim, assignedProver, proverFee). 5) Victim's USDC is transferred to the prover without victim's consent.
prerequisites: Victim has ERC20 approval to AssignmentHook
Attacker obtains prover assignment for same feeToken

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Direct theft of ERC20 tokens from any address that has approved AssignmentHook
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Any proposer who has given ERC20 approvals to AssignmentHook
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Victim can revoke approval; stolen tokens cannot be recovered

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for proposer setting coinbase to a different address when using ERC20 fee payment

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable by monitoring BlockProposed events where coinbase != proposer

threats.title: SgxVerifier Allows Self-Replacement Bypassing Key Rotation and Expiry
threats.hypothesis_description: SgxVerifier._replaceInstance does not check if newInstance == oldInstance. An SGX instance can submit a proof where the new instance address is the same as the old one. This resets validSince to block.timestamp, effectively extending the instance's validity by another 180 days without actual key rotation, defeating the side-channel protection intended by mandatory key rotation.
threats.investigation_rationale: The INSTANCE_EXPIRY of 180 days is a security measure to limit exposure from potential SGX side-channel attacks. If an instance can indefinitely extend its validity without rotating keys, this protection is nullified.

proof_of_signal.pattern: instances[id] = Instance(newInstance, uint64(block.timestamp)) without checking newInstance != oldInstance

preconditions: SGX enclave can sign proofs specifying newInstance = current instance address
Instance has not yet expired (within 180-day window)

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: validSince reset to block.timestamp allows infinite extension
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No validation that new instance differs from old instance

analysis_questions: Does the SGX enclave program enforce key rotation at the software level?
Can proof.data be crafted with newInstance == oldInstance?
What is the practical security impact of bypassing key rotation?
validation_requirements: Verify that _replaceInstance accepts newInstance == oldInstance
Confirm validSince is reset, extending validity
Check if off-chain SGX software enforces rotation independently

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: 1) SGX instance A is registered at time T with validSince=T. 2) At time T+170 days (near expiry), instance A proves a block with newInstance=A. 3) _replaceInstance sets instances[id] = Instance(A, T+170_days). 4) Instance A now valid for another 180 days from T+170. 5) Repeat indefinitely.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Degraded SGX security model; compromised key usable indefinitely
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Protocol security relies on SGX key freshness
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Owner/watchdog can delete instances if compromise detected

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable by monitoring InstanceAdded events where old == new

threats.title: Guardian Proofs Blocked During Proving Pause Prevents Emergency State Correction
threats.hypothesis_description: TaikoL1.proveBlock() is gated by both whenNotPaused and whenProvingNotPaused modifiers. GuardianProver.approve() calls TaikoL1.proveBlock() when the approval threshold is met. This means guardians—the highest-authority tier intended to be the ultimate arbiter—cannot submit corrective proofs when proving is paused. The protocol has no separate guardian-only proving path, creating a deadlock scenario where pausing prevents the very mechanism needed to resolve the issue that caused the pause.
threats.investigation_rationale: Guardians are designed as the emergency backstop for the protocol. Blocking their ability to prove during a proving pause defeats this purpose and could extend protocol stalls indefinitely.

proof_of_signal.pattern: proveBlock has whenProvingNotPaused modifier; no alternative path for guardian-tier proofs

preconditions: Proving is paused via pauseProving(true)
Guardians need to submit a corrective proof for a disputed block
No alternative guardian proving function exists

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: whenProvingNotPaused modifier blocks all proofs including guardian tier
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: No guardian-tier exception in the proving pause logic
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No separate proveBlock path or modifier exception for TIER_GUARDIAN

analysis_questions: Can the owner unpause and guardians prove atomically?
What is the maximum delay between pausing and being able to submit guardian proofs?
Could this deadlock prevent protocol finality indefinitely?
validation_requirements: Confirm GuardianProver.approve calls TaikoL1.proveBlock which checks whenProvingNotPaused
Verify no alternative guardian proving path exists
Assess impact on block verification chain when proving is paused

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: 1) Malicious prover submits invalid proof. 2) chain_pauser pauses proving to prevent further damage. 3) Guardians accumulate approvals but cannot submit the corrective proof because proveBlock reverts with L1_PROVING_PAUSED. 4) Protocol stalls: can't verify new blocks, can't fix invalid proof. 5) Must unpause to fix, but unpausing also allows the malicious prover to act again.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Protocol stall blocks all verification; provers' bonds locked; L2 chain progression halted
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Owner must unpause; but no guarantee malicious actors won't exploit the unpause window

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for guardian proving during proving-paused state

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Observable via reverted guardian approval transactions

threats.title: Proposer Can Predictably Avoid Expensive Proving Tiers via Observable Randomness
threats.hypothesis_description: The minimum proof tier for a block is determined by meta_.difficulty = keccak256(block.prevrandao, numBlocks, block.number), which is fully deterministic given observable on-chain state. A proposer can compute the resulting minTier before submitting a transaction and only propose blocks when the cheaper tier (e.g., SGX instead of SGX_ZKVM) is required. This allows systematic evasion of the protocol's random high-security tier sampling.
threats.investigation_rationale: On mainnet, 0.1% of blocks should require SGX_ZKVM tier (more expensive). If proposers can selectively skip these blocks, the high-security sampling becomes ineffective, degrading the protocol's security model that relies on random audits.

proof_of_signal.pattern: meta_.difficulty = keccak256(abi.encodePacked(block.prevrandao, b.numBlocks, block.number)); all inputs observable before tx submission

preconditions: Proposer can compute the tier before submitting (all inputs are public/observable)
Multiple proposers exist so blocks requiring expensive tiers can be skipped by one proposer and potentially left for others
No enforcement that every numBlocks value must be proposed by a specific proposer

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Deterministic randomness from block.prevrandao + numBlocks + block.number; proposer can precompute
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: getMinTier(rand % 1000 == 0) returns SGX_ZKVM only 0.1% of the time; proposer can avoid these
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No mechanism to force proposers to propose blocks requiring expensive tiers

analysis_questions: What percentage of blocks would actually require SGX_ZKVM in practice if proposers game the system?
Can a single proposer monopolize block proposals to entirely avoid expensive tiers?
Does the permissionless nature (if proposer == address(0)) allow anyone to fill skipped slots?
validation_requirements: Compute tier distribution for sequential numBlocks values to confirm predictability
Assess economic incentive: cost of SGX_ZKVM proving vs waiting for next block
Verify that skipping a numBlocks value doesn't permanently block it

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: 1) Proposer monitors upcoming (prevrandao, numBlocks, block.number) tuples. 2) Computes minTier for each. 3) Only proposes blocks where minTier is cheap (SGX). 4) Blocks requiring SGX_ZKVM are either delayed or proposed by others with expensive proving costs. 5) Protocol's random high-security sampling effectiveness is reduced from 0.1% to ~0%.
prerequisites: Proposer can choose when to submit proposals

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Reduced security audit frequency; potential for undetected invalid state transitions
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All protocol users relying on the multi-tier security model
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Guardian proofs still serve as backstop

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for proposer tier gaming

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Statistically detectable by analyzing tier distribution over time

threats.title: Proving Window Indefinitely Extended via Repeated Pause/Unpause by chain_pauser
threats.hypothesis_description: The proving window check in _checkProverPermission uses max(ts.timestamp, lastUnpausedAt) + provingWindow*60 >= block.timestamp. Both pauseProving(false) (via LibProving) and TaikoL1.unpause() reset lastUnpausedAt. A chain_pauser who repeatedly toggles provingPaused (true->false) or the contract pause state resets lastUnpausedAt, extending the assigned prover's exclusive proving window indefinitely and preventing any other prover from submitting proofs for first transitions.
threats.investigation_rationale: If the assigned prover colludes with chain_pauser, they can monopolize the proving window indefinitely, preventing honest provers from submitting alternative proofs and potentially stalling the verification chain.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _checkProverPermission uses max(_ts.timestamp, _state.slotB.lastUnpausedAt) for window calculation; pauseProving(false) resets lastUnpausedAt

preconditions: chain_pauser role is set (resolved via AddressManager)
chain_pauser repeatedly toggles pause state
Assigned prover intentionally does not prove within the original window

initial_code_evidence.suspicious_location: LibProving.sol lines 73-78 (pauseProving resets lastUnpausedAt) and lines 303-316 (_checkProverPermission window calc)
initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: lastUnpausedAt used as lower bound of proving window start; can be repeatedly reset
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: No cap on how many times or how late lastUnpausedAt can be reset relative to block proposal time
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No maximum proving window duration anchored to block proposal time

analysis_questions: How frequently can chain_pauser toggle the pause state?
Does the cooldown window in verification also use lastUnpausedAt, compounding the delay?
Can this be used to permanently prevent verification of specific blocks?
validation_requirements: Confirm that repeated pause/unpause resets lastUnpausedAt each time
Verify that max(ts.timestamp, lastUnpausedAt) produces an ever-extending window
Test that non-assigned provers are permanently blocked while window is extended

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: 1) Block B proposed with assigned prover P at time T. 2) Proving window = T + 60 minutes (SGX tier). 3) P does not prove. 4) At T+59min, chain_pauser calls pauseProving(true) then pauseProving(false), resetting lastUnpausedAt to T+59min. 5) New window: max(T, T+59min) + 60min = T+119min. 6) Repeat before each window expiry. 7) No other prover can ever submit proof for first transition of B.
prerequisites: Collusion between chain_pauser and assigned prover

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Block verification stalled; provers' bonds locked; L2 chain progression halted
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All L2 users and honest provers
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Owner removes chain_pauser role or upgrades contract

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for proving window extension via repeated pause toggling

technical_investigation_focus.gas_analysis_needed: Two pauseProving calls per extension (~50K gas each)
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable by monitoring ProvingPaused events frequency

threats.title: TaikoTimelockController getMinDelay Override Allows Instant Execution for TIMELOCK_ADMIN_ROLE
threats.hypothesis_description: TaikoTimelockController.getMinDelay() returns 0 for callers with TIMELOCK_ADMIN_ROLE, completely bypassing the 7-day timelock delay. Deployment scripts grant TIMELOCK_ADMIN_ROLE to the securityCouncil. If this role is compromised, an attacker can schedule and execute governance operations (contract upgrades, address manager changes, fund extraction) in the same block with zero delay.
threats.investigation_rationale: The timelock's purpose is to give users time to react to governance changes. Returning 0 delay for admin negates this protection. The deployment grants this role to securityCouncil, making it a high-value target. The UpgradeScript.s.sol shows that upgrades go through timelock.schedule -> timelock.execute, and with delay=0 these execute atomically.
threats.config_evidence: DeployOnL1.s.sol line: _timelock.grantRole(_timelock.TIMELOCK_ADMIN_ROLE(), securityCouncil); confirms securityCouncil gets admin role
preconditions: Attacker obtains TIMELOCK_ADMIN_ROLE (compromise of securityCouncil)
Attacker also has PROPOSER_ROLE and EXECUTOR_ROLE (securityCouncil has all three per deployment)

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Complete timelock bypass; all governance protections nullified
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No minimum delay floor; no multi-step approval for admin operations

analysis_questions: What operations can be executed via timelock that would cause maximum damage?
Is there any on-chain mechanism to detect or prevent instant execution?
Can the admin role itself be transferred or revoked through the zero-delay path?
validation_requirements: Confirm that schedule + execute with delay=0 completes in same transaction
Trace all contracts whose ownership is the timelock to assess blast radius
Verify deployment script role assignments

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: 1) SecurityCouncil key(s) compromised. 2) Attacker calls timelock.schedule with malicious payload (e.g., AddressManager.setAddress to replace bridge with attacker contract) with minDelay=0. 3) Attacker calls timelock.execute immediately. 4) Bridge contract replaced; attacker drains bridged assets.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Total protocol takeover; all bridged assets, tokens, and contract logic at risk
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: None once malicious operations are executed

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test verifying timelock delay cannot be bypassed

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable via timelock events but too late to prevent

threats.title: Assigned Prover Permanently Locked Out After Proving Window Expires or After First Proof
threats.hypothesis_description: In _checkProverPermission, when tid==1 and ts.tier==0 and inProvingWindow, only the assignedProver can submit. In ALL other cases, the assignedProver is explicitly BLOCKED (L1_ASSIGNED_PROVER_NOT_ALLOWED). This means: (1) after the proving window expires, the assigned prover can never prove their own block, even at a higher tier; (2) for non-first transitions (tid>1), the assigned prover is always blocked. If the assigned prover's first proof is contested, they cannot submit a higher-tier proof to defend their correct transition, and a third-party prover who resolves it becomes ts.prover, causing the assigned prover to lose half their liveness bond during verification.
threats.investigation_rationale: This creates an economic unfairness: an assigned prover who proved correctly can be contested and then permanently locked out of re-proving. A third party who submits the higher-tier proof (confirming the original was correct) captures the bond rewards while the assigned prover loses liveness bond.

proof_of_signal.pattern: else branch: if (isAssignedPover) revert L1_ASSIGNED_PROVER_NOT_ALLOWED(); blocks assigned prover from all non-initial-window proving

preconditions: Assigned prover has already submitted first proof within window
Someone contests the first proof at the same tier
Assigned prover wants to submit a higher-tier proof to defend their transition

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Unconditional block of assignedProver in else branch; no exception for higher-tier re-proving
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: After contest, ts.tier > 0 means the if-branch (tid==1 && tier==0) is never entered; assigned prover always hits the revert
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No path for assigned prover to defend their correct proof at a higher tier

analysis_questions: Does the economic model compensate the assigned prover sufficiently despite the lockout?
Can this be griefed to systematically steal liveness bonds from honest provers?
Is there a Sybil avenue where the assigned prover uses a different address?
validation_requirements: Trace the full economic flow: assigned prover proves -> contested -> third party resolves
Calculate net economic impact on assigned prover
Verify that Sybil proving (using a second address) is viable as a workaround

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: 1) Assigned prover P proves block correctly at SGX tier. 2) Attacker contests with different (incorrect) transition. 3) P cannot re-prove at SGX_ZKVM tier (blocked by _checkProverPermission). 4) Third party Q submits SGX_ZKVM proof confirming P's original transition. 5) Q becomes ts.prover. 6) During verification: ts.prover(Q) != assignedProver(P), so P loses livenessBond/2. 7) Attacker profits from contest reward structure.
prerequisites: Assigned prover has proven first transition

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Assigned prover loses up to 125 TKO (half liveness bond) per griefed block despite being correct
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Prover can use Sybil address as workaround; economic loss per block is bounded

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for assigned prover attempting higher-tier re-proof after contest

technical_investigation_focus.capital_requirements: Contest bond (500 TKO for SGX tier)

threats.title: Verification Bond Payout Uses Stale blk.livenessBond After Top-Tier RETURN_LIVENESS_BOND When ts.prover != assignedProver
threats.hypothesis_description: In the current code, when a top-tier proof triggers RETURN_LIVENESS_BOND, blk.livenessBond is set to 0 and transferred to assignedProver. During subsequent verification in verifyBlocks, bondToReturn = ts.validityBond + blk.livenessBond. If for some reason the top-tier proof ts.prover != blk.assignedProver, bondToReturn -= blk.livenessBond >> 1 (which is 0 >> 1 = 0). However, if RETURN_LIVENESS_BOND was NOT triggered but guardian proves with different prover address, bondToReturn includes the full livenessBond. The concern is about potential future tier configurations where a non-guardian tier has contestBond == 0 (isTopTier) and validityBond > 0, combined with RETURN_LIVENESS_BOND, which could lead to double-payment: livenessBond returned via RETURN_LIVENESS_BOND path + validityBond paid out via verification.
threats.investigation_rationale: While current tier configurations make this safe (guardian tier has validityBond == 0), the code doesn't structurally prevent the scenario. If tier configurations change, the bond accounting could break.
deployment_assumptions: Current tier providers have guardian as only top-tier with validityBond=0

proof_of_signal.pattern: RETURN_LIVENESS_BOND path: transfers livenessBond and zeros blk.livenessBond; only gated by isTopTier (contestBond==0), not by validityBond==0

preconditions: A tier configuration exists where contestBond == 0 (isTopTier) AND validityBond > 0
Proof data contains RETURN_LIVENESS_BOND magic value
This is NOT the case with current MainnetTierProvider but could occur after tier provider upgrade

initial_code_evidence.suspicious_location: LibProving.sol lines 176-184 (RETURN_LIVENESS_BOND) and LibVerifying.sol lines 149-158 (bond payout)
initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: RETURN_LIVENESS_BOND guarded by isTopTier only; no check that validityBond == 0
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: If validityBond > 0, both livenessBond (via RETURN) and validityBond (via verification) are paid out
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No assertion that top-tier validityBond == 0 when RETURN_LIVENESS_BOND is used

analysis_questions: Can tier provider be upgraded to introduce a tier with contestBond=0 and validityBond>0?
Would the RETURN_LIVENESS_BOND + verification payout exceed the contract's TKO balance?
Is there a structural guarantee that top-tier always has validityBond=0?
validation_requirements: Check all tier provider implementations for contestBond/validityBond combinations
Simulate full bond flow with hypothetical tier where contestBond=0 and validityBond>0
Verify contract solvency under the combined payout scenario

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: 1) Tier provider upgraded to include a tier with contestBond=0, validityBond=500 TKO. 2) Prover proves at this tier with RETURN_LIVENESS_BOND proof data. 3) blk.livenessBond (250 TKO) transferred to assignedProver, blk.livenessBond=0. 4) During verification, bondToReturn = 500 + 0 = 500 TKO transferred to ts.prover. 5) Total payout: 250 + 500 = 750 TKO, but only 250 (liveness) + 500 (validity) = 750 was deposited. This is actually balanced. The concern is if the validity bond wasn't fully deposited due to reward netting.
prerequisites: Custom tier provider with non-standard bond configuration

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Potential protocol insolvency if bond accounting breaks under non-standard tier config
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All provers and the protocol treasury

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for RETURN_LIVENESS_BOND with non-zero validityBond top tier

threats.title: SgxVerifier Skips All Proof Verification During Contesting Allowing Unverified Contests
threats.hypothesis_description: SgxVerifier.verifyProof returns immediately without any verification when _ctx.isContesting is true. This means ANY address can contest an SGX-tier proof without actually possessing valid SGX attestation or signature. The contester only needs to post the contestBond (500 TKO on mainnet). While this is architecturally intentional (contests are economic bets), it means the SGX security guarantee does not apply to contests, reducing the bar for griefing attacks.
threats.investigation_rationale: The contestable rollup design allows contests without full proof verification, but this means the cost of contesting a valid SGX proof is only the contestBond, not the cost of obtaining an actual SGX instance. This could incentivize speculative or griefing contests.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (_ctx.isContesting) return; — skips all verification during contesting

preconditions: Block has been proven at SGX tier
Attacker has contestBond amount of TKO (500 TKO on mainnet)
Contester submits proof at same tier with different transition

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Early return on isContesting bypasses all SGX signature verification
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Anyone with TKO can contest SGX proofs without any SGX capability
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No partial verification during contests; no contest-specific SGX check

analysis_questions: Is the contest bond sufficient to deter speculative contesting?
What is the expected cost/benefit of contesting every SGX proof?
Could systematic contesting delay block verification beyond acceptable thresholds?
validation_requirements: Calculate economic incentive: contestBond loss probability vs verification delay impact
Assess maximum number of blocks that can be simultaneously contested
Verify that higher-tier proof resolution correctly slashes invalid contesters

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: 1) Honest prover proves block at SGX tier with correct transition. 2) Attacker contests with arbitrary (wrong) transition, posting 500 TKO contestBond. 3) SgxVerifier.verifyProof skips verification (isContesting=true). 4) Block verification blocked until higher-tier proof resolves contest. 5) Attacker loses 3/4 of contestBond (375 TKO) when resolved, but delays verification by cooldown period of higher tier. 6) Repeat across many blocks to delay L2 finality.
prerequisites: Tolerance for loss of 375 TKO per invalid contest

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Griefing cost ~375 TKO/block; delays verification by hours per block
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All L2 users waiting for block finality
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Guardian proofs can resolve; attacker eventually runs out of TKO

threats.test_coverage_gap: No economic analysis of systematic contesting cost
